fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

INTRODUCTION

The application before the Court is an application made by the applicant wife, MS CAPELLO (“the wife”), to enforce orders made by Hartnett J on 25 February 2025 requiring that the first respondent husband, MR CAPELLO (“the husband”) make payments to her by way of partial property settlement and spousal maintenance (“the interim orders”).
The wife’s application seeks to invoke the power in section 90AE of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) to compel third party corporations in which the husband holds interests to take steps directed to furnishing the husband with the funds required to fulfil his obligations pursuant to the interim orders. Section 90AE is part of Part VIIIAA of the Act, which contains provisions dealing with orders and injunctions against third parties.
The wife’s enforcement application is made in the context of ongoing proceedings before the Court with respect to property settlement, spousal maintenance and parenting.

BACKGROUND

The wife was born in 1980 and is aged 45. The wife is engaged full-time in homemaking duties and is reliant on Centrelink benefits.
The husband was born in 1976 and is aged 50. The husband is employed by a corporate entity known as S International Pty Ltd, which is the main trading entity within a group of entities collectively known for the purposes of these proceedings as the Capello Group, and which operates a business known as BB Company. The evidence of the husband and his brother, MR Y (“the husband’s brother”), who gave evidence on behalf of the third parties, is that the husband works on a part-time basis. The precise nature of the husband’s role is not clear from the evidence before the Court. His documents simply list his occupation as “director.” His brother’s evidence is that he assists with “organisational and managerial tasks.”
The parties commenced cohabitation in 2006 and were married in 2009. There is one child of the marriage, X born in 2017, presently aged eight.
The parties physically separated on 16 May 2021 after a period of cohabitation of approximately 15 years. The husband asserts that they had been separated under the one roof since April 2020. They are not divorced.
The wife and X remain residing in the former matrimonial home at Q Street, Suburb R, South Australia (“the former matrimonial home”), which is registered in the sole name of the husband. It is common ground that the husband pays the mortgage instalments, rates, and other outgoings with respect to the former matrimonial home as they fall due, though the wife alleges that he has also made unilateral withdrawals from the mortgage in the period following the parties’ separation and that he has purported to invoice her for some of the expenses he has paid. The husband pays child support, education expenses and private health insurance premiums for the parties’ child.
The primary business activity of the Capello Group, according to the evidence of the husband’s brother, is the manufacturing and sale of products. As I have mentioned, the main trading entity within the group is S International Pty Ltd. The husband and his brother are directors of this company, as was the husband’s late father, who died in 2021.
The husband’s nephew (the son of the husband’s brother), MR AA, (“the husband’s nephew”) and the husband’s mother, MS CC (“the husband’s mother”) were also made directors of S International Pty Ltd (and various other corporate entities within the Capello Group) while the present application was pending before the Court. The shareholders of S International Pty Ltd are the estate of the husband’s late father (“the estate”) and another corporate entity within the Capello Group by the name of S Investments Pty Ltd.
The interim orders made by Hartnett J on 25 February 2025 provided, relevantly, as follows:
By way of partial property settlement, and pursuant to sections 80(1) and 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and within thirty (30) days of this order, the Respondent do pay or cause to be paid to the trust account of Tolis & Co Lawyers, for and on behalf of the Applicant Wife (“the Applicant”) a lump sum of $100,000.
Pursuant to section 74 of the Act, the Respondent do pay, or cause to be paid, to the Applicant the sum of SIX THOUSAND DOLLARS ($6,000) per month by way of interim spousal maintenance with such payments to be made directly to the Applicant’s nominated bank account commencing on the first Monday following the date of these Orders and on the first Monday of each month thereafter.
The reasons for judgment of Hartnett J[1] record that her Honour was satisfied (for the purpose of the interim applications then before the Court) of the following:
(a) The husband has many assets and/or financial resources available to him and/or in his control which hold a significant value. He had not provided sufficient discovery at the time of the hearing before her Honour, nor valuations sufficient to enable a quantification of the value of the entities in which he has an interest.[2]

(b) Even if only the assets held by the parties personally, as quantified by the wife, were to be considered, the interim property payment sought by the wife and ultimately ordered was considerably less than that which the wife would ultimately receive.[3]

(c) The husband had income, property and financial resources available to him to enable the meeting of this payment.[4]

(d) The husband was reasonably able to support the wife given his financial circumstances and capacity which included “probably his shareholdings; access to cash at bank; and company funds including unpaid present entitlements.”[5]

(e) The husband was “a director of various of the entities and, as claimed by the wife, beneficially and otherwise entitled to an interest in most of the entities.”[6]

An application made by the husband to stay the interim orders was refused by me on 19 September 2025.[7]
It is common ground that the husband has not complied with either of the orders outlined above. He asserts that he lacks capacity to do so from his personal resources. The wife alleges that the husband has ignored requests for compliance made by her solicitors. The husband adduced no evidence to refute this allegation.
According to calculations undertaken on behalf of the wife, as at the date of the hearing, the amount owing pursuant to the partial property order was $109,712.88 including interest, and the arrears with respect to the spousal maintenance order totalled $82,278.10 including interest. The total owed to her by the husband as at the date of the hearing was therefore $191,990.98. That amount will continue to increase for as long as it remains outstanding, by virtue of the ongoing nature of the spousal maintenance liability and increase accruing on the sums payable pursuant to both orders.[8]

THE WIFE’S APPLICATION

The relief sought by the wife (as updated by way of minute of orders sought which was annexed to her case outline) was (relevantly) as follows:
That pursuant to Section 90AE(2)(a) of the Family Law Act 1975 (“the Act”), the Second Respondent, [S2 Pty Ltd], do all things and sign all documents as may be necessary to forthwith place the property situated at and known as [D Street], [Suburb E] in the State of South Australia, being the whole of the land comprised and described in Certificate of Title Register Book Volume [...] Folio [...] (“the [Suburb E] Property”), on the public market for sale with such Agent and upon such terms and conditions as may be agreed between the parties, and failing such agreement in writing within fourteen (14) days, as may be determined by the proper officer of the Real Estate Institute of South Australia or their nominee, AND the Second Respondent do thereafter do all things necessary to direct and instruct its conveyancer to apply the proceeds of sale as follows:
firstly, in payment of the Sales Agent’s commission and all expenses of sale pursuant to any Sales Agency Agreement;
secondly, in payment of conveyancing expenses and adjustments of rates and taxes;
thirdly, in payment of any liabilities currently recorded as owed by the Second Respondent, [S2 Pty Ltd], in its most recently lodged Financial Statements ON THE CONDITION THAT evidence of any such liability be provided to the Applicant Wife prior to payment being made;
lastly, half of the net balance then remaining be paid to the Third Respondent, [S Holdings Pty Ltd] AND the Second Respondent, [S2 Pty Ltd], do declare, and make payment of, a fully franked dividend to the said Third Respondent to give full effect thereto.
That forthwith upon receipt of the dividend referred to in paragraph 1(d) hereof, and pursuant to Section 90AE(2)(a) of the Act, the Third Respondent, [S Holdings Pty Ltd], do all things and sign all documents as may be necessary to declare, and make payment of, a fully franked dividend to the Respondent Husband in such sum as is equivalent to his 50% interest in the net equity of the said entity.
That upon receipt of the payment made to him from the Third Respondent in accordance with these Orders, the Respondent Husband do forthwith do all things necessary to cause and direct the application of such payment as follows:
firstly, to the immediate payment of the sum of $100,000 (with interest accrued thereon) to the Tolis & Co Lawyers Account for, and on behalf of, the Applicant Wife to remedy his breach of paragraph 2 of the Orders of 25 February 2025 UPON NOTING THAT such amount is presently calculated to be in the order of $109,712.88 inclusive of interest;
secondly, to the immediate payment to the Applicant Wife of such amount that is required to satisfy the total spousal maintenance payable to her as at the date of such payment, in addition to interest accrued thereon UPON NOTING THAT such amount is presently calculated to be in the order of $78,000 plus an interest component of approximately $4,278.10 (total of $82,278.10); and
lastly, any balance of the funds then remaining be deposited into the Trust Account of Tolis & Co Lawyers for the purposes of facilitating the Respondent Husband’s ongoing compliance with the monthly payments of spousal maintenance due to the Applicant Wife pursuant to paragraph 3 of the Orders of 25 February 2025 for the period that those funds so allow.
That for the purposes of paragraphs 1, 2 and 3 of these Orders, respectively:
and pursuant to Section 90AE(2)(a) of the Act, the Second Respondent, [S2 Pty Ltd], do:
all things and sign all documents necessary to authorise and direct the Agent and conveyancer appointed in respect of the sale of the [Suburb E] property to liaise with the Applicant Wife and her solicitors regarding the progress of the sales campaign and settlement, respectively, UPON NOTING that these Orders may otherwise be provided to the said Agent and conveyancer and stand as evidence of such authority and direction;
at all times follow the advice and recommendations of the appointed Agent for the sale of the [Suburb E] property;
provide a copy of these Orders to the conveyancer instructed to effect settlement of the sale of the [Suburb E] property forthwith upon their engagement AND do thereafter cause written confirmation of the same to be provided to the Applicant Wife via her solicitors;
keep the Applicant Wife informed, via her solicitors, of the progress of the sales campaign and settlement of the sale of the [Suburb E] property, respectively, AND for such purposes, do further provide to her:
details of the conveyancer appointed to effect settlement of the sale of the said property;
disclosure of any offers received from prospective purchasers within 24 hours of receipt;
a copy of the proposed Contract for Sale in relation to the [Suburb E] property within 24 hours of its preparation; and
a copy of the proposed Settlement Statement prepared in relation to the sale of the [Suburb E] property; and
be restrained and an injunction be hereby granted restraining it from:
accepting any offer received, or entering into a Contract for Sale, in relation to the [Suburb E] Property without the prior written consent of the Applicant Wife or order of this Honourable Court;
authorising the conveyancer instructed to effect settlement of the sale of the [Suburb E] property to complete settlement in accordance with the Settlement Statement provided to the Applicant Wife without her consent first had and obtained;
providing instructions for the application of the proceeds of the sale of the [Suburb E] property in any manner other than as set out herein, or in any manner that would otherwise compromise the entitlements of the Applicant Wife in accordance with these Orders UNLESS in compliance with joint correspondence signed by all parties’ solicitors; and
drawing down on, disposing of, or otherwise depleting, the Respondent Husband’s share of the net proceeds of the sale of the [Suburb E] property without the consent of the Applicant Wife first had and obtained AND unless in accordance with the terms permitted by these Orders; and
the Respondent Husband do:
forthwith provide, to the Applicant Wife, written confirmation and/or evidence of his receipt of the funds to be paid to him in accordance with paragraph 2 of these Orders; and
pursuant to Section 114(1)(e) of the Act, be restrained and an injunction be hereby granted restraining him from drawing down on, applying, withdrawing or otherwise depleting, any funds received by him pursuant to these Orders in any manner other than as permitted herein without the Applicant Wife’s written consent first had and obtained, or prior Order of this Honourable Court.
That for the purposes of paragraph 3 hereof, the Respondent Husband do make payment to such bank account/s as may be nominated by the Applicant Wife's solicitors for, and on her behalf, with the details of the said accounts to be provided forthwith following the making of these Orders.
That the Respondent Husband do, in his personal capacity or otherwise, pay, discharge, and indemnify the Applicant Wife from, and keep her forever indemnified in relation to, any liabilities that may arise as a result of the transactions undertaken pursuant to these Orders.
S2 Pty Ltd and S Holdings Pty Ltd (collectively “the third parties”) are the second and third respondents to the proceedings respectively.
At the time of the making of the interim orders and at the time the wife brought her enforcement application, the officeholdings and membership of the second and third respondents were as follows:
(a) With respect to the second respondent:
(i) The husband and his brother were the directors;

(ii) The husband was the sole secretary; and

(iii) The third respondent and a company within the Capello Group by the name of S International Pty Ltd each held 12 of the 24 issued shares.

(b) With respect to the third respondent:

(i) The husband and his brother were the directors;

(ii) The husband was the sole secretary; and

(iii) The husband and his brother each held 12 of the 24 issued shares.

In or around March 2026, while the present application was extant, changes were made to the directorship of a number of entities within the Capello Group. Most relevantly for present purposes, the husband’s nephew became a director of both the second and third respondents, and the husband’s mother became a director of the second respondent.
The second respondent is the registered proprietor of a commercial property at D Street, Suburb E, South Australia (“the [Suburb E] property”).
The financial statements for the financial year ending 30 June 2025 record that the second respondent held net equity of $125,751.18 and cash at bank of $191,916.49. The evidence of the husband’s brother is that the cash at bank held by the second respondent fluctuates in accordance with rental payments and expenses relating to the Suburb E property and tax. He deposed that accounts payable for the second respondent stood at $30,219.66 as at 10 March 2026. The best available evidence of the value of the Suburb E property is the value in the rates notice issued in February 2025, which was $1,325,000.
According to the evidence of the husband’s brother, the primary liability of the second respondent is an intercompany loan owed to S International Pty Ltd, which was utilised by the second respondent to purchase the Suburb E property (“the Suburb E property loan”). More will be said about this loan later in these reasons.
The 2025 financial statements with respect to the third respondent were not before the Court, and nor was there evidence of their contents. The wife alleged that they had not been provided to her and nor had the 2025 financial statements for various other entities in which the husband holds interests. It is not clear whether those documents have yet been prepared.
As 30 June 2024, the third respondent held net equity of $23,805.29, cash at bank of $37,759.41, and a 50 per cent shareholding in the second respondent. The evidence of the husband’s brother was that as at 28 August 2025, the balance of the third respondent’s bank account was $24,007.34.
By order made with their consent on 19 September 2025, the second and third respondents (together with a number of other entities within the Capello Group which are not relevant for present purposes) were joined to the proceedings for the limited purpose of determination of an Application in a Proceeding that had been filed by the wife on 4 July 2025 (including any subsequent amendments to that application). At the time of the making of the orders of 19 September 2025, the relief sought by the wife by way of enforcement of the interim orders insofar as it related to members of the Capello Group was contained within the Application in a Proceeding filed on 4 July 2025.
By the time of the hearing to which these reasons relate, the applications filed by the wife had been amended, such that the relief sought with respect to the third parties was contained within an Amended Enforcement Application filed on 18 February 2026 and the Application in a Proceeding filed on 4 July 2025 was no longer pressed. At the hearing, senior counsel for the third parties expressly declined to take issue with the extension of the joinder for the related purpose of determination of the relief sought by the wife in the different format of the Amended Enforcement Application.
The husband and the third parties each sought the dismissal of the enforcement application.

DOCUMENTS RELIED ON

The wife relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Enforcement Application filed 18 February 2026;

(b) Affidavit of Ms Capello filed 4 July 2025; and

(c) Case outline filed 18 March 2026.

The husband relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 3 March 2026;

(b) Affidavit of Mr Capello filed 31 July 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Mr Capello filed 3 March 2026;

(d) Case outlined filed 12 September 2025.

The third parties relied on the following documents:
(a) Amended Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 16 March 2026;

(b) Affidavit of Mr Y filed 15 September 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Mr Y filed 16 March 2026; and

(d) Case outline filed 18 March 2026.

By order made on 19 September 2025, the operation of rule 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) was dispensed with and the annexures to the affidavits referred to above were admitted into evidence.
Although each and every aspect of the evidence relied upon by the parties has not been referred to in these reasons, I have had regard to the totality of the evidence before the Court and the submissions of each of the parties.

THE APPLICABLE LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Subsection 105(1) of the Act provides the source of power to enforce orders. It provides that “[s]ubject to this Part, to the regulations, and to the applicable Rules of Court, all decrees made under this Act may be enforced by any court having jurisdiction under this Act.”
In McMillan & McMillan,[9] Tree J provided an overview of the relevant authorities and the applicable principles, which can be summarised as follows (citations omitted):
(a) The use of the word “may” in section 105 establishes a judicial discretion to enforce or refuse to enforce the relevant order.[10]

(b) It is only facts or circumstances arising since the date of the order sought to be enforced that will inform the discretion to be exercised pursuant to section 105 of the Act.[11]

(c) The question for the Court’s consideration is whether in all the circumstances it is inequitable to enforce the order.[12]

(d) The consideration of whether it would be inequitable to enforce the order involves general notions of fairness and is not limited to circumstances where an equitable remedy arises.[13]

(e) The onus upon showing that it is not equitable to enforce the order is upon the party so contending.[14]

(f) Delay in bringing an application for enforcement is a relevant consideration.[15]

Ultimately, prima facie, a party is entitled to the benefit of an order and orders should be enforced.[16]
In my view, no aspect of the evidence before the Court would lead to the conclusion that the interim orders ought not be enforced. The issue for determination is whether this ought to be done in the manner sought by the wife in the present application.
The wife’s application for orders directed to the third parties is brought pursuant to section 90AE of the Act, which is in the following terms:
90AE Court may make an order under section 79 binding a third party
(1) In proceedings under section 79, the court may make any of the following orders:
(a) an order directed to a creditor of the parties to the marriage to substitute one party for both parties in relation to the debt owed to the creditor;

(b) an order directed to a creditor of one party to a marriage to substitute the other party, or both parties, to the marriage for that party in relation to the debt owed to the creditor;

(c) an order directed to a creditor of the parties to the marriage that the parties be liable for a different proportion of the debt owed to the creditor than the proportion the parties are liable to before the order is made;

(d) an order directed to a director of a company or to a company to register a transfer of shares from one party to the marriage to the other party.

(2) In proceedings under section 79, the court may make any other order that:
(a) directs a third party to do a thing in relation to the property of a party to the marriage; or

(b) alters the rights, liabilities or property interests of a third party in relation to the marriage.

(3) The court may only make an order under subsection (1) or (2) if:
(a) the making of the order is reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage; and

(b) if the order concerns a debt of a party to the marriage—it is not foreseeable at the time that the order is made that to make the order would result in the debt not being paid in full; and

(c) the third party has been accorded procedural fairness in relation to the making of the order; and

(d) the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order; and

(e) the court is satisfied that the order takes into account the matters mentioned in subsection (4).

(4) The matters are as follows:
(a) the taxation effect (if any) of the order on the parties to the marriage;

(b) the taxation effect (if any) of the order on the third party;
(c) the social security effect (if any) of the order on the parties to the marriage;

(d) the third party’s administrative costs in relation to the order;
(e) if the order concerns a debt of a party to the marriage—the capacity of a party to the marriage to repay the debt after the order is made;
Note: See paragraph (3)(b) for requirements for making the order in these circumstances.

Example: The capacity of a party to the marriage to repay the debt would be affected by that party’s ability to repay the debt without undue hardship.
(f) the economic, legal or other capacity of the third party to comply with the order;

Example: The legal capacity of the third party to comply with the order could be affected by the terms of a trust deed. However, after taking the third party’s legal capacity into account, the court may make the order despite the terms of the trust deed. If the court does so, the order will have effect despite those terms (see section 90AC).
(g) if, as a result of the third party being accorded procedural fairness in relation to the making of the order, the third party raises any other matters—those matters;

Note: See paragraph (3)(c) for the requirement to accord procedural fairness to the third party.
(h) any other matter that the court considers relevant.
The wife relies in particular on subsection 90AE(2).
The following sections of the Act are also relevant to the wife’s application:
90AA Object of this Part
The object of this Part is to allow the court, in relation to the property of a party to a marriage, to:

(a) make an order under section 79 or 114; or

...

that is directed to, or alters the rights, liabilities or property interests of a third party.
90AB Definitions
In this Part:

...

third party, in relation to a marriage, means a person who is not a party to the marriage.[17]
90AC This Part overrides other laws, trust deeds etc.
(1) This Part has effect despite anything to the contrary in any of the following (whether made before or after the commencement of this Part):
(a) any other law (whether written or unwritten) of the Commonwealth, a State or Territory;
(b) anything in a trust deed or other instrument.
(2) Without limiting subsection (1), nothing done in compliance with this Part by a third party in relation to a marriage is to be treated as resulting in a contravention of a law or instrument referred to in subsection (1).
Section 90AE “confer[s] jurisdiction on, relevantly, [this Court] in respect of property settlements between the parties to a marriage including property in which a party to the marriage has an interest even if title to that property is vested in another person or body.”[18]
In Federal Commissioner of Taxation v Tomaras,[19] Keifel CJ and Keane J in the High Court of Australia emphasised that that the power of the court under Pt VIIIAA to make an order directed to a third party is not at large, and that power to make an order under s 90AE(1) is conditioned by s 90AE(3). Such an order may be made only if, among other things, the making of the order is reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage; and the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order. Subsection 90AE(3) imposes the same conditions with respect to relief under subsection 90AE(2). Those “other things” include, relevantly, the court’s satisfaction that the order takes into account the matters mentioned in subsection (4), as required by subsection (3)(e).
Plainly, to the extent (if any) that section 90AE has application to an enforcement application (which is discussed below), the matters set out in subsections 90AE(3) and (4) would be highly relevant to the question of whether the Court should exercise the discretion conferred by subsection 105(1) by way of an order made under subsection 90AE(2). The subparagraphs in subsection 90AE(3) are conjunctive, so that each one must be satisfied before the Court can make an order.[20]
In Russo & Russo & Ors,[21] O’Brien J in the Family Court of Western Australia held that the requirement in s 90AE(3)(e) that the order take the enumerated matters into account, is distinguishable from the requirement elsewhere in the Act that, in considering whether to make a particular order, the court take into account specified matters. The latter, his Honour held, requires the Court to have regard to particular matters in exercising what is a broad discretion, and permits considered dismissal of the matters specified even if they are arguably relevant to that exercise; while the former requires an identifiable reflection in the order and its effect of the consideration of the matters specified. I respectfully agree with and adopt this interpretation.
In submissions in opposition to the wife’s application, the husband emphasised that he is not in sole control of the second and third respondents. Counsel for the wife did not suggest otherwise. Indeed, were the entities in question completely controlled by the husband, it would not likely have been necessary for the wife to resort to the provisions of Part VIIIAA.[22]

DISCLOSURE ISSUES

Although it is common ground that the husband has provided some financial disclosure, the wife alleged that his disclosure had been deficient and that he had failed to comply properly with his ongoing obligation of full and frank disclosure. Amongst the wife’s allegations with respect to disclosure was a failure to provide statements for bank accounts held by the various entities in which he has an interest.
During the hearing of the application, counsel for the wife demonstrated various other areas in relation to which the husband had failed to comply with his disclosure obligations. These examples are discussed throughout these reasons. Although the husband claimed that “all financial information [had] been shown,” when given the opportunity to produce evidence to refute the wife’s allegations, he was unable to do so.
The obligations cast upon the husband with respect to disclosure are set out in section 71B of the Act, which relevantly provides as follows:
71B Duty of disclosure

Duty of disclosure in proceedings
(1) Each party to a proceeding relating to financial or property matters of a marriage (other than proceedings on appeal) has a duty to the court and to each other party to give full and frank disclosure, in a timely manner, of all information and documents relevant to:
(a) for a party to the marriage--the issues in the proceeding that relate to financial or property matters of the marriage; or
...
(2) The duty under subsection (1) applies from the start of the proceeding and continues until the proceeding is finalised.
...

Relevant information and documents
(8) A party's duty to disclose information and documents is a duty to disclose information known to the party and documents that are or have been in the possession or under the control of the party.

(9) A party's duty to disclose information and documents includes any information or documents prescribed by the applicable Rules of Court for the purposes of the duty.

There are also clear and extensive disclosure obligations outlined in Part 6.1 of the Rules and in numerous authorities.[23] Of particular relevance to the present matter is the obligation set out in subrule 6.06(2), which includes an obligation full and frank disclosure of (relevantly):
...

(b) any vested or contingent interest in property;
(c) any vested or contingent interest in property owned by a legal entity that is fully or partially owned or controlled by a party;

(d) any income earned by a legal entity fully or partially owned or controlled by a party, including income that is paid or assigned to any other party, person or legal entity...

The husband, as a director (and, to a more limited extent, where he is a shareholder) of the relevant corporations, holds various rights to access the records of those corporations.[24] The husband’s brother’s evidence is that the husband has viewing access to the bank accounts of the Capello Group. The husband is required to disclose the financial circumstances of the second and third respondents and various other entities within the Capello Group.
Notwithstanding this, it is apparent that the husband has been, as alleged by the wife, “hiding behind the corporate entities” as an excuse for his failure to comply with his disclosure obligations. For example, on 18 October 2025, the husband wrote to the wife’s solicitors saying “I am unable to access or supply the bank statements requested for the listed entities, as those entities have not granted permission or provided them to me.”[25] When, during the hearing, it was pointed out to the husband that he was making extremely serious allegations against members of his family if he was suggesting that he was being blocked from access to documents and information to which he was legally entitled, he hastily retreated from such a suggestion, leaving his failure to comply with his obligations unexplained.
Furthermore, as correctly submitted by counsel for the wife, she is entitled to disclosure of changes or developments with respect to everything that falls within the husband’s disclosure obligations “in real time.” Failing to provide disclosure as to his financial circumstances and those of the entities in which he holds interests as they occur constitutes failure by the husband to meet his obligations.
It is apparent from the reasons of Hartnett J dated of 25 February 2025[26] that the issue of the adequacy of the husband’s disclosure was a live issue at the time of the hearing before her Honour. Specific orders directed to ensuring compliance by the husband with his ongoing obligations as to disclosure were made by her Honour. Although it would appear uncontroversial that the husband subsequently provided all or most of the individual documents specifically itemised in her Honour’s disclosure orders, unfortunately, over a year later, deficiencies in the husband’s level of compliance with his disclosure obligations continue to loom large in the proceedings.
The potential for serious outcome-related consequences for a party who has failed to comply with their obligations of disclosure is well known. A note to subsection 71B(2) sets out actions that may be taken by the Court in the event of non-disclosure as follows:
(a) take the failure into account when making an order under section 79 (alteration of property interests);

(b) make any orders with respect to costs or security for costs against the person that the court considers just, having regard to the failure;

(c) make any orders with respect to disclosure that the court considers appropriate;
(d) if an order made by the court is contravened--impose sanctions under section 112AD;
(e) punish the person under section 112AP for contempt;

(f) stay or dismiss all or part of the proceedings.
If it is found at trial that the husband has failed to comply with his obligation of full and frank disclosure, this may entail significant consequences for his case and the outcome of the spouse parties’ property settlement proceedings. In Weir & Weir,[27] the Full Court of the Family Court of Australia (as the Court was then known) (“the Full Court”) held that:
once it has been established that there has been a deliberate non disclosure ... then the Court should not be unduly cautious about making findings in favour of the innocent party. To do otherwise might be thought to provide a charter for fraud in proceedings of this nature.[28]
The husband submitted that he is a self-represented litigant but correctly acknowledged that this is not an excuse for failure to comply in full with his disclosure obligations. In any event, the husband was represented by experienced family lawyers for over a year after the property proceedings were commenced and as I have mentioned, the issue of disclosure was a live issue at the hearing before Hartnett J. It is inconceivable that the husband has been unaware of his fundamental obligations in this regard.
The third parties, as parties to the proceedings, are also subject to disclosure obligations, although those obligations are more limited in scope than those imposed on the spouse parties. Subsection 71B(1)(b) provides that:
Duty of disclosure

Duty of disclosure in proceedings
(1) Each party to a proceeding relating to financial or property matters of a marriage (other than proceedings on appeal) has a duty to the court and to each other party to give full and frank disclosure, in a timely manner, of all information and documents relevant to:
...
(b) for any other party to the proceeding--so much of the party's financial circumstances as are relevant to the issues in the proceeding that relate to financial or property matters of the marriage.
A number of issues linked to the inadequacy of the disclosure provided to the wife during the course of both the enforcement application and the underlying financial proceedings were raised during the course of the hearing and will be discussed throughout these reasons.

DISCUSSION

Before embarking on consideration of the matters outlined in section 90AE of the Act, it is necessary to consider whether section 90AE can be invoked in the circumstances of the present application at all. The answer to that question is not straightforward and involves a number of different but related considerations.

Applicability of section 90AE to a spousal maintenance obligation

On a plain reading of its terms, subsection 90AE(2), the operation of which is expressly limited to “proceedings under section 79” cannot be invoked for the purpose of enforcement of a spousal maintenance application. Consistently with this interpretation, in Lin & Ruan,[29] an order requiring the sale of a property partly owned by the husband and partly owned by his mother to enforce an order made against the husband in favour of the wife that was properly characterised as a spousal maintenance order was held not to be a valid exercise of the power in section 90AE.
Counsel for the wife did not dispute that section 90AE has no application to orders for spousal maintenance. He submitted, however, that the proposed orders providing for the sale of the Suburb E property and the payment of dividends would have the effect of placing a pool of funds in the hands of the husband against which the spousal maintenance order could then be enforced. It was submitted that once the power under section 90AE had been exercised with respect to the “primary issue” of the partial property order, which had been made pursuant to section 79, the work of section 90AE would be done, and the spousal maintenance order could be enforced against the husband with respect to those funds.
It is unquestionably the case that the spousal maintenance order could be enforced against funds received by the husband by way of dividend payment and that the husband could be ordered to pay any sum so received by him to the wife in compliance with that order. This does not, however, address the fact that what the Court is being asked to do is to make orders binding the third parties which are directed to effecting the payment of the dividend to the husband, in part for the purpose of the payment of spousal maintenance.
In my view, even if it is accepted that the power under section 90AE may be invoked for the purpose of enforcement of an order made pursuant to section 79 (an issue which is discussed below), to the extent that the orders sought would require the payment of funds to the husband in excess of the sum required to satisfy the order made under section 79, they are beyond the power conferred by section 90AE.

Applicability of section 90AE to an enforcement application

The third parties submitted that by reason of its limitation to “proceedings under section 79,” section 90AE could not be used for the purpose of an application for enforcement under section 105. They also argued that even if there were some scope for use of section 90AE in the enforcement context, it could not be for the purpose of enforcing against third parties who were not parties to the underlying orders an obligation that had been imposed directly upon a party to the marriage.
On behalf of the wife, it was submitted that the relief sought was, properly characterised, “under section 79” in circumstances in which the order sought to be enforced had been made pursuant to section 79. It is also arguably relevant that the present application is made not as a standalone application but in the context of ongoing proceedings pursuant to section 79.
Interpretation of section 90AE is aided by consideration of section 90AA, which is set out above, and which relevantly provides that the object of Part VIIIAA is to allow the court, in relation to the property of a party to a marriage, to “make an order under section 79” (being the same language used in the heading to section 90AE) that is directed to, or alters the rights, liabilities or property interests of a third party. Subsection 90AE(2) itself is prefaced with the words “in proceedings under section 79....” The application of the subsection is therefore contingent upon the relevant order being made in proceedings under section 79. The question is therefore whether the orders I am asked to make are properly characterised as being “in proceedings under section 79” notwithstanding that the application in which they are made is an enforcement application, and the power to make enforcement orders is contained within section 105 and not section 79.
In Mackah & Mackah,[30] in the context of an application to enforce orders that had been made pursuant to section 79, O’Brien J held that the proceedings were not brought under section 79 but rather sought to enforce orders already made under section 79, and as a consequence, section 90AE had no application.
In Lin & Ruan, Austin J (with whom Watts & Ainslie-Wallace JJ agreed), held that “[t]he first pre-condition for an order under s 90AE is that it be made in the context of an application for a property settlement order.” The application in Lin & Ruan was for an order to enforce a spousal maintenance obligation. As a consequence, the Court was not required to address the question of whether, had the underlying obligation sought to be enforced been imposed pursuant to section 79, this “first pre-condition” would have been satisfied. In light of the ongoing proceedings between the spouse parties in the present matter, it is at least arguable that the wife’s application is made “in the context of an application for a property settlement order.”
In Toma & Doyle (No 2),[31] the wife sought to join a number of third parties to proceedings under section 79. The husband opposed the joinder, in part on the basis that the orders sought against the third parties were largely enforcement orders, sought in the event that the husband failed to comply with an order for payment of a lump sum. The wife submitted that the joinder was necessary because once the underlying section 79 proceedings had concluded, then arguably the wife could not thereafter seek relief under section 90AE in the event that the husband failed to make the payment of any part of the sum awarded in her favour. Justice Strum recorded that none of the counsel who appeared before him was able to cite any authority where section 90AE was invoked after orders were made under section 79, for the purpose of enforcing same.
His Honour noted that there may be other options available to the wife for pursuing the proposed further respondents in relation to the husband’s interests in the group of entities in question following the making of an order under section 79, including not only various mechanisms under the Rules, but also section 90AF, which is part of Part VIIIAA, and which provides that in proceedings under section 114, a court may make an order or grant an injunction that “directs a third party to do a thing in relation to the property of a party to the marriage.” His Honour noted in this regard, that “proceedings under section 114” would include, as expressly provided for at subsection 114(3), proceedings for “an injunction in aid of the enforcement of a decree.”
Counsel for the wife in the present matter submitted that in order for section 90AE to have “teeth,” it was necessary that it be available for enforcement of orders made under section 79. It was also submitted that the consequence of acceptance of the third parties’ position would be that the interim orders would be rendered unenforceable by dint of the fact that the third parties’ involvement was required to release the property interests held by the husband, which, it was submitted, would put the property of the husband beyond reach, being an outcome the Court could not endorse. While there is merit in these submissions, and it may well be that section 90AE is limited in its ability to achieve its intended purpose by reason of the limitations on its applicability in the enforcement context, I am tentatively inclined towards respectfully agreeing with the view expressed by O’Brien J in Mackah & Mackah.
The meaning of “proceeding” was discussed by the Full Court in Gilford & Cavaco.[32] In that case, the majority (Harper and Brash JJ) examined the definition of “proceeding” in section 7 the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) (“FCFCOA Act”), the relevant part of which is “a proceeding in a court, whether between parties or not, and includes an incidental proceeding in the course of, or in connection with, a proceeding, and also includes an appeal.” That definition is similar, but not identical, to the definition of “proceeding” in section 4 of the Act, which is “a proceeding in a court, whether between parties or not, and includes cross-proceedings or an incidental proceeding in the course of or in connection with a proceeding.”
The majority in Gilford & Cavaco, after examining authorities with respect to the meaning of “proceeding”[33] concluded that an application for leave to institute a spousal maintenance application was a separate “proceeding” from an extant application for property settlement orders, notwithstanding that they both related to the same “matter” or “justiciable controversy.” In my view, there is no relevant difference between the definition of “proceeding” in the FCFCOA Act and that in the Act, and the same reasoning applies to the distinction between a primary application pursuant to section 79 and an application for enforcement of an order previously made under that section, notwithstanding the related nature of the two applications.
I consider that an application under section 105 is “an incidental proceeding in the course of or in connection with a proceeding” and is, itself, a “proceeding” in the relevant sense. As a consequence, the relief sought by the wife pursuant to section 90AE is not sought “in proceedings under section 79” but in a separate (albeit related) proceeding under section 105.
This view is supported by the fact that (similarly to the property and spousal maintenance applications that were before the court in Gilford & Cavaco), proceedings with respect to orders under section 79 and proceedings with respect to enforcement (including interim enforcement applications such as the present) are described as separate proceedings and appear in separate limbs of the definition of “matrimonial cause” in section 4 of the Act.
Proceedings under section 79, being “proceedings between the parties to a marriage with respect to the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them...” fall within subsection (cb) of the definition of “matrimonial cause;” while an enforcement application falls within subsection (f) of that definition, which is “any other proceedings (including proceedings with respect to the enforcement of a decree or the service of process) in relation to concurrent, pending or completed proceedings of a kind referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (eb)....”

Applicability of section 90AE to interim orders

Noting that the order made under section 79 sought to be enforced by the wife is interim in nature, it is necessary to consider whether section 90AE applies to interim orders.
In Russo & Russo & Ors, O’Brien J considered whether an order made on an interim basis can properly be said to be reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to “effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage.” His Honour noted the following:
(a) The power under section 79, whether exercised on an interim or final basis, is to make orders “altering the interests of the parties to the marriage” in property; the expression “division of property” is not used.[34]

(b) An interim order made pursuant to section 79 must be capable of variation or reversal without resort to section 79A or an appeal, and “capable of alteration at any time prior to, or as part of, the final exercise of the section 79 power.”[35]

His Honour then said:
Can it, therefore, properly be said that such an interim order “effect[s] a division of property between the parties to the marriage”? In my view, it cannot. Rather, such an order effects an interim alteration of the property interests of the parties in particular items of property, in a manner capable of variation or reversal. The words used in s 90AE, in my view, refer instead to the division of property which occurs consequent to the making of a final order for alteration of property interests. Further, in my view the expression “effect a division of property between the parties” itself connotes finality. In expressing that view I acknowledge that it may be open to argue that there is a relevant distinction between “a division of property between the parties” and “the division of property between the parties.”
(emphasis in original)
The Full Court in Lin & Ruan suggested, but did not expressly decide, that an interim adjustment of property interests may attract the operation of section 90AE. Their Honours did not make reference to the analysis undertaken by O’Brien J in Russo & Russo & Ors.
I note that section 90AE does not in fact require that the order made under that section itself “effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage,” but rather that the order be order be reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, for that purpose. It may well be the case that an interim order can be “directed to” the purpose of effecting a division of property between the parties, even if the division itself will not be determined or ordered until a later time. It is also arguable that an interim property payment can be properly be characterised a component of the ultimate “division” of property (even though that component may be altered at a later date) with the consequence that an order directed to that component can be characterised as being reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to “effect a division of property” in the relevant sense.
However, there will be circumstances in which it is not possible to determine whether an order is reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage until such time as it is known what form that division will take.
It must be noted that the present application is in fact further removed from the ultimate “division of property between the parties to the marriage” than an interim order made under section 79, because it is, as I have discussed, not an application made under section 79 at all, but one made pursuant to section 105. This weighs against the conclusion that section 90AE may be invoked in the manner sought by the wife in the present application.

Does the application call for the creation of property?

It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that the wife’s application sought to create property of the parties, or more particularly the husband, that does not presently exist. It is well-established that the creation of property not otherwise in existence is an impermissible use of the power in section 90AE.
In B Pty Ltd and Ors & K and Anor (“B Pty Ltd”),[36] the primary judge had permitted the wife to amend her application to seek that the directors of corporate trustees of discretionary trusts of which the husband was a beneficiary cause the relevant trusts to make a capital distribution in favour of the husband so that such sums could be paid to the wife. The husband and the third parties, who had opposed the amendments on the basis that the powers contained in section 90AE could not found the orders sought by the wife, successfully appealed.
In B Pty Ltd, the Full Court noted that the orders proposed by the wife sought “not to divide, but to increase the present property of the parties.”[37] The Full Court further held that:
... In our view, all that s 90AE(2)(b) does, of relevance to the wife’s claim here, is to enable the court to adjust the property interests of a third party for the purpose of effecting a division of the present property of the parties to the marriage, between those parties. Only in the sense that altering interests may leave a bundle of rights or interests that are consequent upon the alteration, may the exercise of power create interests, but these “new” interests will be the residue of what already existed at law. ...
...
In our view, the correct conclusion was that, as the wife set out her proposed claim, she did not show that the power conferred by s 90AE could arguably be engaged. Any order made pursuant to s 90AE(2)(b) must be for the purpose of effecting a division of property between the parties. The order that the wife proposed was for the purpose of increasing the property of the parties, by an unknown amount and on unknown principles.
It would be impossible, based upon the wife’s deposition, to identify what the wife said were the facts material to a cause of action which would lead to an order that the trustees of a discretionary trust distribute to one of a group of beneficiaries. The amendment would have joined the third parties to an unformulated claim to which they would have had great difficulty responding. That Morgan J did not address this issue is, in our view, an appealable error.

(Emphasis in original)
In AC and Ors & VC and Anor,[38] which also related to a discretionary trust, this time an application to bring forward the vesting date and require the trustee to distribute the corpus and income of the trust amongst its specified beneficiaries, the primary judge had posed the question “whether [the court] can take steps to bring the property entitlement into existence.” In that case, the husband was not found to be in control of the trust but was found to have had significant involvement with the trust and its assets, although the genesis of those assets were not attributable to him, and the primary judge found that the husband had a realistic prospect of gaining control of the trust assets in the future after the completion of the proceedings.
The primary judge in AC and Ors & VC and Anor distinguished B Pty Ltd on the basis that although the trust in question was discretionary, because it had no appointor or guardian, the spouses, as specified beneficiaries, each held a fixed existing property entitlement, which the Court was bringing into tangible form. As such, the primary judge held, unlike the situation in B Pty Ltd, property was simply being redistributed amongst the parties and third parties, and was not being created.
The Full Court was satisfied that Part VIIIAA could be used to require a trustee (including a third party trustee) to bring forward the vesting date of a trust for the “ancillary” purposes of valuing an irrevocable entitlement to ultimately share in the trust fund and distributing that share to the party entitled, and that these powers can be exercised even at the expense of third party interests, provided that the requirements in (relevantly) ss 90AE(3) and (4) are met, and the order, if made under s 79, is “just and equitable.” Their Honours were satisfied that (subject to the applicable legislative safeguards) such an exercise of power met the requirement that it be made “in order to effect a division of the property of parties to a marriage” or to make orders ancillary to that division.[39]
Whilst the entitlements of beneficiaries of discretionary trusts, as in B Pty Ltd, and shareholders of private companies, as in the present case, have many differences, just as beneficiaries of discretionary trusts hold no entitlement to demand the payment of distributions, shareholders lack the power to require the payment of dividends. Subject to the terms of the constitution of the particular company,[40] decisions as to whether to declare dividends and related decisions as to matters such as timing and quantum, fall to the board of directors.[41] However, in my view, the fixed, quantifiable (and, in this case, beneficially held) proprietary interest of a shareholder in a company is fundamentally different to the undefined and contingent interest held by a beneficiary of a discretionary trust, whose interest in property extends only to an equitable chose in action in the form of a right to due administration of the trust.[42] The facts of the present matter are, in my view, more akin to those in AC and Ors & VC and Anor than those in B Pty Ltd.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife in the present matter that having regard to the membership of each of the second and third respondents, 50 per cent of the shares in the second respondent were owned by the third respondent and 50 per cent of the shares in the third respondent were owned by the husband, ergo the husband was entitled to 25 per cent of the net assets of the second respondent.
Furthermore, it was submitted that the husband effectively held a further 25 per cent of the net value of the assets of the second respondent because:
(a) the remaining 50 per cent of the shares in the second respondent were held by S International Pty Ltd, and 50 per cent of the shares in that company formed part of the residue of the husband’s late father’s estate, of which the husband is one of two beneficiaries; and

(b) the other 50 per cent of the shares of S International Pty Ltd were held by S Investments Pty Ltd, 50 per cent of the ownership of which “flows back” to the estate.

A colour coded version of a diagram depicting the Capello Group structure was relied on to demonstrate this assertion.[43]
In addition, it was submitted on behalf of the wife, in reliance on this analysis, that in circumstances in which the Suburb E property loan (if it is enforceable, being a matter discussed later in these reasons) would be repaid to S International Pty Ltd, the ultimate result would be that those funds would “flow back” to the estate, such that ultimately the loan was a “mirage” pursuant to which the husband owed the funds in question to himself.
Counsel for the wife submitted that ultimately, when the various interests in the Capello Group were properly analysed and traced, the husband and his brother jointly enjoyed ownership and control between them. Counsel emphasised that all that was sought was the release of the husband’s existing interest in property, and not the acquisition of any property owned by any third party. It was submitted that the payment of dividends is a function of the corporate structure, that the husband already held the entitlements in question, and that all that the wife sought was that they be realised. It was further submitted that the orders sought by the wife would result in no change in beneficial ownership of any property and that rather, the declaration of dividends was sought as a means of aligning the legal and beneficial ownership of the relevant property.
It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that the approach of taken by the wife reflected an overly simplistic approach to a complex corporate and trust structure. The husband likewise challenged the interpretation of the flow of wealth within the Capello Group propounded by the wife.
I am not persuaded that the effect of the interests held by the husband within the Capello Group is quite as straightforward as the submissions made on behalf of the wife would suggest. Firstly, the “flowing back” of 50 per cent of the shareholding in S International Pty Ltd held by S Investments Pty Ltd would appear on the evidence presently before the Court to involve interests held by a number of other entities, including at least one discretionary trust, against whom no interlocutory relief is presently sought.
Furthermore, even if the shareholdings in question were all directly beneficially held and unaffected by trust interests, as Barwick J observed in In Re Webster,[44] “under the general law it is well established that a shareholder does not have any legal or equitable interest in the assets... of the company. Even where a shareholder owned almost all the shares of a company, he had no legal or equitable right or interest in the company’s assets.” In the family law context, Carew J observed in Rigby & Kingston[45] that “a shareholder does not by its shareholding have a legal or equitable interest in the assets of the corporate entity. The property interest it has is in the shares.”
Nonetheless, unlike a beneficiary of a discretionary trust, who, as I have observed, holds no proprietary interest beyond an equitable chose in action in the form of a right to due administration of the trust,[46] the husband does hold interests in property within the Capello Group in the form of shares, and the value of those shares would likely (although not certainly) be determined by reference to the net value of the assets of the relevant companies.[47] In these circumstances, I consider that the application made by the wife is, properly characterised, an application to convert and realise the value of property interests already held by the husband, and I do not consider that it impermissibly seeks to create additional property.

Are the identified issues with the application of section 90AE determinative?

It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that the areas of legal uncertainty identified in the discussion above warrant caution, and that this weighs against the determination of these issues at an interlocutory stage where the evidence before the Court is incomplete, partly disputed and wholly untested. I do not consider that, as a general rule, the Court should shy away from determining legal issues involving novelty or uncertainty on an interim basis if the interests of justice require it.
However, in my view, it is not necessary to conclusively determine these areas of uncertainty at this stage of the proceedings because even if I were satisfied that the Court has the power pursuant to 90AE to grant the relief as sought by the wife, for the reasons which follow, I would not be persuaded to make the orders as sought by the wife prior to a final hearing.

The requirements of section 90AE

In AC and Ors & VC and Anor, the Full Court emphasised that the legislative safeguards for third parties, who will be subject to or affected by the exercise of the Part VIIIAA powers, must be observed in order for the exercise of power to be valid.[48]
In the present matter, the second and third respondents (together with other members of the Capello Group) were joined to the proceedings for the purpose of determination of the wife’s application for orders against them in aid of enforcement of the orders made by Hartnett J,[49] being an application of which they were provided notice and in relation to which they were heard. There was no suggestion that they had not been provided procedural fairness. The requirement of subsection 90AE(3)(c) is therefore satisfied.
The matters outlined in subsections 3(b), 4(c) and 4(e) do not arise on the facts of this matter and I have not identified any matters requiring consideration under subsection 4(h).
The balance of the matters requiring consideration pursuant to section 90AE are considered below.

Is the making of the order reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage?

In AC and Ors & VC and Anor, the Full Court discussed the uncertainty of the meaning of the words “in relation to the marriage” in the context of the phrase “alters the rights, liabilities or property interests of a third party in relation to the marriage” in subsection 90AE(2)(b). Their Honours referred with approval to the following passage from the judgment of O’Ryan J in Hunt & Hunt & Lederer & Ors:[50]
When s 90AE(2) is read in conjunction with s 90AE(3), s 79, and Part VIIIAA generally, it is clear that what is contemplated is not some arbitrary invasion of the rights of a third party but an alteration of those rights where they are sufficiently connected to the division of the property between parties to a marriage.
The Full Court in AC and Ors & VC and Anor went on to hold that the phrases “reasonably necessary” and “reasonably appropriate and adapted” which appear in subsection 90AE(3)(a) (and in subsection 90AF(3)(a), which was the provision under consideration in that case), should be read, not separately, but together with the balance of the paragraph as providing “the requisite sufficient connection between the making of the order ... in relation to a third party and the core of the marriage and matrimonial causes power.”[51] Their Honours held, however, that it was nonetheless necessary to consider whether the order under contemplation was “necessary” in a practical sense to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage.
In Russo & Russo & Ors, O’Brien J emphasised that the limitation expressed in s 90AE(3)(a) requires careful attention in each case, and that the singular nature of the identified purpose, to effect a division of property between the spouse parties, must be borne in mind. His Honour also held that:
(a) “Reasonably necessary” does not mean “essential” without any qualifier; rather, the making of the order will meet that criterion if there is no clear and reasonably practicable means of achieving the same purpose;[52] and

(b) The expression “reasonably appropriate and adapted” involves “proportionality testing,” which, in turn, involves satisfaction that the making of the order is suitable to meet the singular purpose, and a consideration of other reasonably practicable means of achieving that purpose without impingement on the rights of the third party in question. Where such other reasonably practicable means are available, the court should not readily be satisfied that orders affecting the rights of third parties should be made.[53]

In my view, the considerations of whether a proposed measure is reasonably necessary and whether it is reasonably appropriate and adapted are intertwined. A step may be rendered necessary or unnecessary by reference to the extent to the alternative available measures are comparatively appropriate and adapted. Similarly, the extent to which a measure may be considered appropriate and adapted may be affected by the extent to which it is necessary.
As highlighted by Harper J in Banduka & Banduka,[54] one aspect of the purpose of Part VIIIAA is to protect the interests of third parties.
It is of some significance that this is not a case in which there is a history of substantial funds having been made available to the husband by the third parties in question for the purpose of the support of the wife or indeed for his own self-support.[55] The husband deposed that throughout the marriage and following separation, his income consisted of predominantly, if not exclusively, of wages and salary, and was not derived from dividends or trust distributions. Counsel for the wife pointed out, however, that the only financial statement filed by the husband in these proceedings, which was filed over two years ago in November 2023, disclosed trust distributions as a source of income.
The evidence of the husband’s brother is that it is has been the practice of the Capello Group for over 60 years to retain and reinvest any profit back into the business and to not pay dividends to shareholders or distributions to beneficiaries. His evidence is that there has never been a dividend paid by the third respondent to either the husband or his brother. The husband’s brother’s evidence as to trust distributions was somewhat contradicted by other evidence given by him as to various distributions which had been declared in favour of the husband by a number of trusts within the Capello Group which are represented as unpaid present entitlements.
Nevertheless, there is no evidence before the Court of a history of the second or third respondents specifically having paid dividends. Even if the evidence of the husband and his brother as to the limited extent to which the husband has received funds other than his employment income from entities within the Capello Group more broadly has been exaggerated, the evidence does not suggest that the third parties themselves have historically provided for the husband’s support, nor that their present resistance to paying dividends in the manner proposed by the wife has been contrived as a means of thwarting her application. Were it otherwise, the relief sought might more readily commend itself as being reasonably appropriate and adapted in the necessary sense.
In Malloy and Ors & Stopford Malloy,[56] albeit in a somewhat different context, the Full Court emphasised the need for care and caution and with respect to issues of proportionality in circumstances in which orders providing for drastic measures are sought and there is doubt as to whether sufficient funds will be realised to meet their objectives.
A major issue going to the proportionality of the orders sought in the present application is the enforceability of the Suburb E property loan. The loan agreement, which was dated 12 January 2017, was in evidence before the Court. It recorded that the sum of $1,007,604.35 was lent by S International Pty Ltd to the second respondent for the purpose of purchase of the Suburb E Property. The terms of the loan required repayment of “principal, accrued [i]nterest at historical market rates, and other costs incurred by the [l]ender” from the sale proceeds.
The husband’s brother deposed that the Capello Group’s accountant had calculated that the total amount that would need to be repaid to the lender, S International Pty Ltd, if the Suburb E property were sold would be $1,866,674.35, made up of the principal sum of the $1,007,604.35, interest of $815,070, and management fees of $44,000.
The wife’s application expressly provides for the proceeds of sale of the Suburb E property to be applied (prior to any dividend being declared) to purposes including:
...payment of any liabilities currently recorded as owed by the Second Respondent, [S2 Pty Ltd], in its most recently lodged Financial Statements ON THE CONDITION THAT evidence of any such liability be provided to the Applicant Wife prior to payment being made...
It was not a matter of dispute that the principal sum owed pursuant to the Suburb E property loan was recorded in the most recently lodged financial statements of the second respondent. Nonetheless, at the hearing, it was argued on behalf of the wife that the loan was statute barred and ought to be disregarded on that basis. In fairness to the wife and her representatives, had the husband provided them with a copy of the loan agreement from the outset of the proceedings in accordance with his disclosure obligations rather than allowing them to learn of its terms only upon service of the affidavit relied upon by the third parties in the days leading up to this hearing, it is likely that this issue would have been raised much earlier.
Pursuant to section 35 of the Limitation of Actions Act 1936 (SA), the period within which an action must be taken is six years.
Where a loan is in the form of a simple liability repayable on demand, in the absence of a demand for repayment, the statutory time limit commences to run from the time of the advancement of the funds.[57] This is to be contrasted with a loan in relation to which the intention of the parties was to postpone the arising of a cause of action for recovery of the debt until a specified event,[58] in which case the period does not commence until the occurrence of that event. In In re Brookers (Australia) Ltd (in liq); Brooker v Pridham,[59] having referred to the general proposition that where there is a simple loan of money, the debt is due and payable immediately and from day to day from the time of the making of the loan, and that the cause of action therefore arises immediately upon the loan of the money, King CJ said:
...If, however, the agreement between the parties is that the loan is repayable only upon the happening of a certain event or upon compliance with a condition precedent to liability, the debt is not immediately due and payable and the cause of action does not arise until the happening of the event or compliance with the condition ...
Clause 3 of the Suburb E property loan agreement provides that:
No periodic principal or Interest repayments are required while the Lender remains solvent. On a sale of the Property, the Borrower must repay the Lender principal, accrued Interest at historical market rates, and other costs incurred by the Lender in full from the sale proceeds.
Clause 4 bears the heading “term” and simply records “N/A.”
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that in circumstances in which there had been no steps taken to enforce the loan and no repayments made towards it since 2017, it was statute barred. Counsel for the wife submitted that the Suburb E property loan was, properly characterised, repayable on demand, in circumstances in which there was no set period of time for repayment provided for in the loan agreement, as expressly recorded in clause 4.
On behalf of the third parties, it was submitted that the correct interpretation of the contract was that, pursuant to clause 3, it was repayable upon the happening of a certain event, being the sale of the Suburb E property, and that event had not yet occurred, with the consequence that the limitation period had not yet commenced.
Senior counsel for the third parties submitted that if the Suburb E property loan were set aside, this would result in a substantial loss to the lender, S International Pty Ltd. That company is not a party to the proceedings, though it is the subject of a pending joinder application, is represented by the same legal representatives as the third parties, and its directors are the husband and his brother. Senior counsel for the third parties, however, submitted that there were other parties potentially affected by any determination that the Suburb E property loan should be disregarded, including five family members who are beneficiaries of a discretionary trust. These issues do not go to the question of whether the loan is in fact statute barred, but are relevant to the impact of the relief sought by the wife in the event that it is.
In my view, the interpretation urged by the third parties, that the Suburb E property loan is repayable on the happening of a fixed event, being the sale of the property, is to be preferred, at least to the extent that I am not persuaded at this interlocutory stage that the loan, which is duly recorded in the financial statements of the second respondent, can be disregarded.
In my view, clause 3 establishes that the loan is repayable upon a fixed event, being the sale of the Suburb E property, and clause 4 merely records that there is not otherwise a fixed or specified term or duration of the loan, rather than that the loan is repayable on demand. This is not a contract in which “nothing is said about repayment.”[60] I consider that clause 3, which includes a specific reference to an event following which the loan will be repayable, can be interpreted as displacing what would otherwise be “an implied contract to repay the money at once.”[61]
If it is accepted (as it has been for the purposes of the present application) that the Suburb E property loan is repayable upon the sale of the Suburb E property, the husband’s brother calculated that taking into account other expenses discussed elsewhere in these reasons (including capital gains tax (“CGT”), sale costs and accounts payable), the net result would be a deficit in the order of $708,102. The third parties asserted, based on these calculations, that that the Suburb E property would need to sell for well over $700,000 more than the value presently attributed to it in the February 2025 rates notice in order to produce any profit such as to enable the payment of a dividend. If the estimated figures applied by the third parties to these calculations are accurate or close to accurate, there would be no utility at all in the orders sought by the wife. Plainly, an order devoid of utility cannot be “reasonably necessary.”
How the “historical market rates” of interest and the “management fees” said to be owed by the second respondent were calculated by the Capello Group accountant was not explained. Nor was the interpretation of the phrase “other costs incurred by the lender” contained within the loan agreement as meaning “management fees.” I accept the submission of counsel for the wife that there may well be enforceability issues associated with the vague obligations outlined in the loan agreement.
Nonetheless, even if only the principal sum were accepted as being owed, and even allowing for some considerable inaccuracy in the calculations relied upon by the third parties (some of which were necessarily estimates), noting that any increase in sale price over the value for the Suburb E property used for the calculations would also result in increases in both CGT and sales commission, it is by no means clear that the sale of the Suburb E property would produce a sufficient surplus to enable the payment of any significant dividend which could ultimately result in funds in the hands of the husband, much less an amount sufficient to render the relief sought against the third parties proportionate to the benefit ultimately derived by the wife.
This is particularly so in circumstances in which, even on the wife’s case, any dividend paid by the second respondent would necessarily be limited to 50 per cent of net equity held by that entity (after payment of all the associated costs and tax), and the same limitation would in turn be applied to any dividend then paid by the third respondent to the husband. It is not the case that the entirety of the net proceeds of the Suburb E property would simply be paid to the wife.
However, irrespective of the status of the Suburb E property loan, there is still significant reason for caution with respect to the proportionality of the relief sought by the wife. As is discussed later in these reasons, the best available evidence of the likely CGT payable upon the sale of the Suburb E property suggests that it will be in excess of $100,000. As submitted on behalf of the third parties, it is significant that this cost alone would likely exceed the quantum of the partial property payment which the wife seeks to enforce, and there is a “mismatch” between the amount she is seeking to enforce and the magnitude of the consequence entailed by implementation of the orders she seeks. That is a significant factor weighing against the conclusion that the proposed orders are proportionate to the outcome they seek to achieve and therefore reasonably appropriate and adapted to that purpose.
As observed by O’Brien J in Russo & Russo & Ors, consideration of whether the relief sought is “reasonably necessary” involves consideration of whether there are reasonably practicable means of achieving the specified purpose, being effecting a division of property between the parties to the marriage.
