This requirement highlights the difficulties with determining an application pursuant to section 90AE at an interlocutory stage, as there remain many unknowns with respect to the other options that may ultimately be determined to be available for the purpose of achieving this end.
As submitted on behalf of the wife (albeit in the context of a different point) the husband could sell his shares in the relevant entities, whether to the other shareholders or otherwise, and thus realise the value of his interests in the Capello Group. That may well be one means by which it is ultimately determined that a just and equitable settlement of property between the spouse parties can be achieved. The evidence presently before the Court as to the details of the options available to the husband in this regard is limited, and includes the absence of the constitution of the third respondent.
A consideration relevant to the extent to which it could be said that the relief sought against the third parties is necessary and/or appropriate and adapted in the relevant sense is the impending inheritance by the husband from the estate of his late father. Probate was granted in favour of the husband and his brother on 29 November 2024, yet the estate is yet to be administered. The executors have attributed a value of $7,730,585.42 to the estate.[62] The husband is one of two residuary beneficiaries. The will provides that he, together with his brother, will inherit the residue of their father’s estate, which largely takes the form of shares in three companies within the Capello Group (being companies other than the second and third respondents).
A claim pursuant Inheritance (Family Provision) Act 1972 (SA) was filed by the husband’s mother on 17 December 2024. The husband’s mother discontinued her claim on 18 May 2025.
The orders made by Hartnett J on 25 February 2025 included the following orders with respect to the estate:
7. The Respondent do inform the Applicant, via her solicitors, of:
(a) any further claim made in relation to, or against, his late father’s Estate pursuant to the Inheritance (Family Provision) Act 1972 (SA) (“the IFP Act”), or any repealed Act in that regard, within 48 hours of receipt of notice of such claim AND do provide a copy of the same to the Applicant’s solicitors forthwith upon receipt thereof; and

(b) the progress of any claim/s made in relation to, or against, his late father’s Estate pursuant to the IFP Act, or any repealed Act AND will provide such update/s to the Applicant’s solicitors monthly unless any significant developments occur in anticipation of such time.

Pursuant to section 114(1)(e) of the Act, the Respondent be hereby restrained and an injunction be granted restraining him from, without providing 30 days’ notice in writing to the Applicant’s solicitors AND the Applicant expressly consenting to the same:
...
(c) dealing with, diminishing, drawing on, and/or depleting, his interest in the Estate of his late father pending final agreement with the Applicant in relation to settlement of property pursuant to section 79 of the Act, save and except to such extent that the same may be required to meet expenses that arise in the ordinary course of business;

(d) signing any document or instrument or doing any act or thing, including but not limited to entering into or signing a Deed of Family Arrangement, that has the effect of compromising or resolving any claim or claims made in relation to, or against, his late father’s Estate pursuant to the IFP Act, or any repealed Act in that regard without providing 30 days’ prior notice to the wife ...

The wife alleged that notwithstanding these orders, the husband had not so much as disclosed the Notice of and Consent to Discontinuance filed with respect to his mother’s claim to her, and that she had been required to obtain that document from the probate office.
There was no evidence before the Court providing any adequate explanation as to why, following the discontinuance, the estate had not been administered in accordance with the husband’s late father’s will, nor demonstrating any meaningful steps taken by the husband, who is one of two executors, to ensure that this occurred. The wife alleged that despite the terms of the orders made by Hartnett J and the husband’s ongoing obligations of disclosure, she had been provided with no meaningful information or updates with respect to the administration of the estate. This circumstance is entirely unsatisfactory.
The evidence of the husband’s brother was that despite the discontinuance of his mother’s claim, there was an agreement “attempt to negotiate a resolution to the issue of [Mr Z’s] Estate outside of the court proceedings.”
The husband deposed vaguely that there had been “discussions and meetings” with respect to the estate from which he had been excluded despite being one of two executors and despite there being no extant claim against the estate, and asserted that unspecified “negotiations between beneficiaries” have “progressed.” At the hearing, he alleged that all he had received was “some hearsay” and “a bit of a threat.”
The case outline filed on behalf of the third parties appeared to suggest that there may be some prospect that the husband’s mother might take further action with respect to the estate. Senior counsel for the third parties (who does not act for the estate) advised the Court at the hearing that there was an “in principle agreement” which could lead to a deed of family arrangement.
There was no evidence before the Court of the details of the purported agreement. Nor was there any evidence to explain why the details of such agreement had not been disclosed to the wife in accordance with Hartnett J’s orders (and the husband’s disclosure obligations more broadly) and then (subject to any steps taken by the wife) finalised. Counsel for the wife alleged that no disclosure had been provided in relation to any such proposed agreement at all and that his client was learning of this development for the first time during the hearing. This was one of the allegations of non-disclosure which the husband was unable to produce evidence to refute despite being given the opportunity to do so.
The husband concerningly sought to attribute his purported exclusion from the unspecified discussions to the existence of Hartnett J’s orders and claimed that this exclusion continued to “complicate” his disclosure obligations. It is not clear precisely what the husband alleged was occurring in this regard. At least one potential interpretation of the husband’s allegation could have potentially serious consequences for those against whom the allegation was made, noting that serious consequences can befall a person who aids and abets a contravention of, or intentionally prevents compliance with, an order made under the Act by a person who is bound by it.[63]
The husband’s brother asserted that it “may become necessary to have [the husband] removed as an executor” because of an asserted “conflict of interest.” The nature of the asserted conflict of interest was not explained, noting that the husband’s fiduciary duties as administrator of the estate, which require the administration of the estate in accordance with the will for the benefit of the beneficiaries, would appear, on the evidence before the Court, to be wholly aligned with obligations as a litigant in these proceedings.
Obviously, if steps were taken which had the effect of reducing the husband’s entitlements in the estate without the knowledge or consent of the wife, that would be a matter of serious concern for the wife and for the Court, whether the husband remained an executor or not. Avoiding such an outcome is the clear and obvious basis for the injunctions imposed by Hartnett J. Members of the husband’s family would be ill-advised to attempt to remove him as executor for the purpose of circumventing the orders of this Court, particularly if, as appears to be the case, they wish to minimise their own involvement in these proceedings.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that as a consequence of the fact that the form of the husband’s inheritance will predominantly be shares in corporate entities within the Capello Group, receipt by the husband of his interest in the estate would not avoid the need for orders to be made against members of the Capello Group providing for the sale of assets in order to realise his interests. Whilst that might be the case, more detailed evidence as to the various options available for dealing with what will ultimately be the husband’s entitlements in the Capello Group more broadly would be relevant to the questions of the necessity and appropriateness of the relief presently sought by the wife.
The evidence also suggests that the husband may have unpaid present entitlements in trusts owing to him, of which he could require payment in order to make payment of the funds owed by him to the wife, though the evidence before the Court with respect to the detail of such entitlements issue is limited. The ability or potential ability of the husband to require (as opposed to “request,” as he deposes to having done) payment of such funds weighs against the conclusion that the relief sought by the wife against the third parties is necessary in the relevant sense.
It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that it was premature to determine a number of aspects of the application before the Court at the interim stage of the proceedings, and that to do so would occasion prejudice for both the husband and the impacted respondents, particularly to the extent that the husband’s ability to retain his interests in the Capello Group was not yet known. Put another way, it was suggested that aspects of the application before the Court would more appropriately be left to be determined at a trial, where the evidence would be more fulsome (and would include valuation and other expert evidence), the evidence could be tested and definitive findings could be made as to the precise nature and value of the husband’s interests in the various entities within the Capello Group. It was further submitted that the longstanding status quo of the asset holdings of the Capello Group should not be disturbed on an interim basis.
Senior counsel for the third parties submitted that if orders of the nature of those sought by the wife were to be contemplated at all, this should only occur at the trial stage, with the third parties having had a fulsome opportunity to put before the Court evidence of the likely prejudice to them (which would require expert evidence from valuers and an accountant). There is, in my view, considerable force in these submissions. The risk of undue prejudice arising from irreversible decisions with significant consequences being made on the basis of a truncated hearing, inexact estimates and untested lay evidence is high.
In AC and Ors & VC and Anor, the Full Court said that it would have been of assistance if the primary judge had provided in-depth analysis of how the value of all the parties’ assets was to be distributed between them, before apparently determining that her proposed orders were reasonably necessary to effect the proposed property division. The same is true in the present case, though of course it is not possible to engage in such an analysis without a trial and without fulsome evidence. This is a factor weighing against the appropriateness and wisdom of seeking to determine whether the orders sought are “reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage” prior to a final hearing at a time when the evidence is, of necessity, incomplete.
I accept the submissions made on behalf of the wife that the evidence before the Court indicates that the interests held by the husband in the Capello Group comprise a substantial part of the assets available for distribution between the parties. If a just and equitable distribution of property between the parties cannot otherwise be effected, there will ultimately need to be a means of extracting the value of the husband’s interests to the extent necessary to provide the wife with ultimate entitlements.[64]
The submission made on behalf of the wife to the effect that the husband cannot simply “hide behind the corporate veil” by leaving his wealth tied up in his interests in the various entities as a means of denying the wife her just entitlements is wholly accurate. It may well be that this ultimately has ramifications for third parties, including but not limited to the second and third respondents. However, in my view, the determination of how the wife’s entitlement to a just and equitable property settlement can best be met, including the mechanics of the associated orders, is a matter best determined at trial where the evidence before the Court will be more comprehensive, that evidence can be tested, and findings can be made.

Is it just and equitable to make the order?

As O’Brien J held in Russo & Russo & Ors, even if satisfied that the making of the order is reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage, the court may only make the order if satisfied that in all the circumstances it is just and equitable to do so. The consideration of justice and equity self-evidently extends to the interests of the relevant third parties. Relatedly, section 90AK of the Act prohibits the Court from making an order that would result in the acquisition of property other than on just terms.
Many of the barriers to a determination that the relief proposed by the wife would be reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage, as outlined above, also preclude a determination that it would be just and equitable to make the orders proposed by her.
I accept and endorse the submission made on behalf of the wife that a just and equitable outcome requires that a party not be permitted simply to hide behind the corporate veil as a means of deflecting claims made against them in circumstances in which their wealth is tied up in corporate and trust structures. It is well established that a party to a marriage is not entitled simply to place assets in the hands of corporate entities, thus removing them from the reach of courts with jurisdiction under the Act.[65] However, the extent and precise nature of the husband’s interests in property and the manner in which they can best be dealt with in order to achieve a just and equitable settlement are, in my view, matters which are best determined at trial and not at an interlocutory hearing based on incomplete and inexact evidence.
It has long been held that the power to make orders which affect the assets of or interests in a company must be used with caution.[66] Whilst the Court’s jurisdiction has been expanded by the enactment of Part VIIIAA, the need for caution has not been attenuated. The provisions of Part VIIIAA cannot be used to deprive third parties of their rights or property simply because to do so would benefit a party to the marriage.[67]
The Explanatory Memorandum to the legislation that enacted section 90AE said: “[t]he provision is intended to apply only to the procedural rights of the third party it is not intended to extinguish or modify the underlying substantive property rights of third parties.”[68] This does not, however, sit easily with the express wording of section 90AA, which provides that a purpose of Part VIIIAA is to allow the court, in relation to the property of a party to a marriage, to make an order that “is directed to, or alters the rights, liabilities or property interests of a third party” (emphasis added).
The characteristics of the particular third party have been recognised as an important consideration in the exercise of the power under section 90AE.[69] In this case, as emphasised by counsel for the wife, it is of significance that the third parties in question are not arms-length strangers but entities within a group controlled by the husband and members of his family.
Counsel for the wife submitted that the Suburb E property had been chosen as the asset held within the Capello Group of which to seek the sale because it was not required for the business operations of the main trading entity. I accept that this is an important consideration.
It is also important to consider, however, that the evidence suggests that the husband made no direct financial contribution to the acquisition of the Suburb E property, and that its acquisition formed part of an investment strategy which included persons other than the husband and which was developed well prior to the separation of the spouse parties. It is not alleged that the second respondent (or, for that matter, the third respondent) is a puppet, sham or alter ego of the husband.[70]
Furthermore, noting the discussion of the distinction between shareholdings and rights to dividends undertaken by Harper J in Franc & Franc (No 7),[71] in the absence of an order of the Court, (and subject to the contents of the constitution of the third respondent, which is not before the Court), the husband has no enforceable right to require the payment of a dividend, and the second and third respondents have no legal obligation to pay dividends or distribute capital or income to the husband.
The submissions made on behalf of the third parties emphasised that they were not parties to the interim orders and were not heard in relation to their making. It was submitted that it would not be just and equitable in those circumstances to enforce the orders against them, nor to visit upon them the consequences of liabilities personal to the husband or the husband’s non-compliance. There is merit in that submission.
It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that there was no evidence of efforts made by the wife to enforce the orders against the husband directly. I do not agree. The original version of the wife’s enforcement application constituted an effort to enforce the orders against the husband. The wife alleged that that application had not been fruitful in large part as a consequence of lack of cooperation and disclosure on the part of the husband. Counsel for the wife tendered correspondence that had been sent to the husband requesting information, which information was not forthcoming,[72] leaving the wife unable to enforce directly against the husband notwithstanding her attempts to do so. Furthermore, as submitted on behalf of the wife, there is no evidence of any attempts made by the husband himself to sell any assets under his control in order to meet his obligations pursuant to Hartnett J’s orders.
In Malloy and Ors & Stopford Malloy, the primary judge had declined to make findings that a property which the wife sought to have sold, which was subject to a mortgage held by the husband’s father and securities taken by a corporate group controlled by members of the husband’s family, had been encumbered so as to quarantine it from claims from the wife or that the securities in question were never to be enforced. Once such a determination had been made, the Full Court held that it was difficult to see how these encumbrances could have been dismissed as not being impediments to steps proposed to be taken with a view to sale. Malloy and Ors & Stopford Malloy is distinguishable from the current matter in that it related solely to enforcement of orders for the payment of spousal maintenance by the husband to the wife and did not attract the operation of section 90AE. Nonetheless, it highlights the importance of such considerations.
The wife in the present matter alleged that actions had been taken by the husband and at least some members of his family and entities within the Capello Group for the purpose of defeating her claim. Plainly, were the third parties or the individuals involved in their operation ultimately found to have been involved in any actions taken to interfere with the husband’s interests and officeholdings or undermine the wife’s claims in these proceedings, this would be a factor of significant relevance to the question of the justice and equity of orders being made against them. The evidence presently before the Court does not presently permit a finding that there has been collusion of this nature.
However, nor is the contrary conclusion readily apparent from the available evidence. This includes, but is not limited to, the evidence of ongoing non-disclosure resulting in the wife not being kept properly informed. By way of example, wife alleged that, despite the husband’s disclosure obligations (and those of the third party), she did not learn of the changes to the directorship of the second and third respondents (and other entities in the Capello Group) until she was served with the affidavit of the husband’s brother filed on 16 March 2026 (only two clear business days prior to the hearing). The husband admitted that he had been aware of these changes and had not informed the wife. This is entirely unsatisfactory. Similarly unsatisfactory is the fact that the wife was not provided with a copy of the loan agreement pertaining to the Suburb E property loan, a document that is highly relevant to the wife’s overall claim and not just the current application, until she was served with the affidavit of Mr Y filed on 16 March 2026, only days ahead of the hearing.
The husband concerningly deposed that he has been informed that “active steps are being considered to remove or replace [him] in certain trustee and director roles, including proposals to appoint additional directors or trustees to represent estate and beneficiary interests.” The wife alleged that any purported exclusion of the husband from the Capello Group was a sham.
The fact that not only have steps been taken to change the directorship of each of the second and third respondents whilst the current application was pending before the Court, but the wife was not advised of this immediately upon it occurring, much less given advance notice, lends credence to the concerns raised by her, as does the husband’s allegation of exclusion from the administration of an estate of which he is one of two executors and against which there is no extant claim.
The evidence of the husband’s brother is that the directorship changes were made “to address the now long-term gap in governance and directors' duties, which have not been adequately fulfilled due to the [husband]'s ongoing part-time involvement and broader absence from participation in the management of the group” and “as part of appropriate succession planning for the group.” Why it had suddenly became necessary to address what was, on his own evidence, a longstanding circumstance, at the very time the present application was pending was not explained. Making these changes while the current application was pending was, at best, unwise. Failing to provide immediate notice to the wife was unacceptable.
A further explanation given for the appointment of the husband’s mother as director was “to ensure that her interests in the estate are properly represented.” In circumstances in which the husband’s mother has withdrawn her claim with respect to the estate and is not a beneficiary under the will, this reference to “her interest in the estate” is very difficult to understand. This is particularly so in light of the fact that the available evidence suggests that some of the entities of which she has become a director are not directly connected to the estate. Even if it were accepted that the husband’s mother has some kind of (unexplained) “interests in the estate,” no explanation was provided as to how her appointment as a director of the entities in question could ensure that such interests were protected. Furthermore, as submitted on behalf of the wife, the appointment of the husband’s mother as a director of multiple entities is somewhat surprising in light of suggestions made by the third parties that her age and state of health caused her to withdraw the Supreme Court claim and give rise to the possible need for a litigation guardian.
The wife also alleged that the third parties had engaged in obstructive behaviour, such as by failing even to respond to correspondence from her solicitor seeking their consent to transactions. No evidence establishing the contrary was adduced.
Notwithstanding these somewhat concerning matters, many of which are issues that cannot be resolved at this interlocutory stage, it would appear to be uncontroversial that the Capello Group is a longstanding structure, the original formation of which was unrelated to the present proceedings. The husband and his brother have been directors of the second and third respondents since 2016, prior to the spouse parties’ separation. The Suburb E property was acquired by the second respondent in 2017 and the Suburb E property loan taken at that time. It was not suggested that the Suburb E property loan was not genuine or that the funds in question had not in fact been provided.

The taxation effect of the order on the parties to the marriage

It would not appear likely that there would be any taxation effect of the orders sought on the wife. There may be a taxation effect on the husband. If the third respondent were to pay a fully franked dividend to the husband, he would be liable for tax on the receipt of that dividend to the extent that his personal tax rate exceeded the company tax rate of 30 per cent. If an unfranked dividend were paid to the husband, it would be fully taxable in his hands. There was no evidence before the Court with respect to this issue.

The taxation effect of the order on the third parties

The taxation effect of the orders proposed by the wife on the second and third respondents is a very significant issue.
It is common ground that a sale of the Suburb E property would result in a CGT liability for the second respondent. The husband’s brother gave evidence that the Capello Group’s accountant had estimated the CGT liability at $103,083 in the event that the Suburb E property sold at the value at which it is recorded in the balance sheet of the second respondent, being the value of $1,325,000 derived from the rates notice issued in February 2025. That estimated sale price is not derived from a sworn valuation and its reliability is questionable. Even if it represented a reasonable approximation of the value of the property at the time it was issued (which is by no means certain), it is now significantly out of date.
In Stephens & Stephens and Anor,[73] the wife, who sought relief pursuant to section 90AE, had led no evidence as to the taxation effects of the proposed orders. The Court was therefore unable to be satisfied that the proposed order will take into account any taxation effects, which prevented the making of the orders sought by the wife.
In the present case, it was submitted on behalf of the third parties that the wife’s proposal did not reflect proper consideration of the taxation position of the affected entities, particularly their capacity to pay the associated tax.
Counsel for the wife properly conceded that there was no evidence before the Court demonstrating the taxation effect on the third parties of the payment of fully franked dividends as sought by her. Counsel explained that this was attributable to lack of knowledge held by the wife with respect to the details of the financial circumstances of the entities in which the husband holds interests, despite her requests for, and entitlement to, information in that regard, including the extent of existing franking credits and details of any loan accounts.
Counsel also proposed that the wife could provide an indemnity akin to an undertaking as to damages, such that any tax consequences could, if subsequently found to be appropriate by the Court, be visited upon her. The prospect that any tax payable by the third parties may, at some future time, upon satisfaction of some undefined criteria, be ordered to be repaid by the wife, does not, in my view, enable the Court to comply with the requirement in subsection 90AE(4)(b) that the Court be satisfied that the order take into account the taxation effect on the third parties. This is particularly so in circumstances in which the evidence adduced on behalf of the third parties suggests that there may be a risk of insolvency which could be compounded by taxation consequences of unknown quantum.
Furthermore, as submitted on behalf of the third parties, were the wife ultimately called upon to reimburse the third parties for the CGT incurred upon the sale of the Suburb E property, this would require her to make a payment greater than the partial property settlement payment which she seeks to enforce. This would be a perverse outcome pursuant to which the third parties would have lost the benefit of ownership of the asset in circumstances in which nothing was ultimately gained by the wife. In addition, the third parties submitted that the offer made by the wife in this regard was “cold comfort” in circumstances in which the means by which she sought to secure her ultimate entitlements was by way of further orders against members of the Capello Group and assets held within that structure.
Similarly, a suggestion that funds could be set aside for the purpose of meeting taxation consequences does not enable the Court to meet its obligation pursuant to subsection 90AE(4)(b), and the need for such a measure and consequent reduction in the quantum of funds ultimately made available to the husband, and subsequently the wife, raises further doubt as to the proportionality of the relief sought, being an issue which I have already discussed.
Counsel for the wife submitted that the Court is not bound by the proposals of the parties and that it was open to the Court to order the payment of unfranked dividends. Whilst it is true that the Court is not bound by the precise proposal of the parties, this suggested alternative approach would not resolve the problem, but rather shift it. Unfranked dividends would be taxable in the hands of the recipients thereof, which in the case of dividends paid by the second respondent would be the third respondent and another entity within the Capello Group, and in the case of dividends paid by the third respondent, would be the husband and his brother.
True it is that the wife is presently at a disadvantage compared with the husband and the third parties when it comes to adducing evidence with respect to matters relating to the financial circumstances of the third parties, and I accept the submission that regard must be had to the impact of the limits to the lack of disclosure on behalf of (at least) the husband on the wife’s inability to adduce evidence. This is an issue that may be considered more fulsomely at trial.
Nonetheless, it is the wife, as the applicant for relief under section 90AE, who bears the onus of adducing evidence sufficient to enable the Court to craft orders which take into account the matters enumerated in subsection 90AE(4), and there are limits to the extent to which the absence of evidence, even where it is the fault of the party against whom relief is sought, can be relied upon as a foundation for a positive conclusion.[74] Ultimately, particularly with respect to the taxation consequences of the orders sought, I accept the submission made on behalf of the third parties to the effect that the wife has adduced insufficient evidence to enable the Court to be persuaded of the matters of which it must be satisfied that the order made takes into account.

The third parties’ administrative costs in relation to the order

The costs associated with the sale of the Suburb E property, all of which would be borne by the second respondent and therefore, even on the basis of uncritical acceptance of the beneficial ownership tracing exercise promoted by the wife, only half of which would ultimately flow through to the spouse parties or either of them, are a significant consideration.[75]
I accept the submission that each aspect of the orders sought by the wife would also likely require the third parties to incur other costs such as accounting costs, and that it is of significance that the full extent of the likely costs to the third parties is not presently quantifiable.
These costs, together with other losses likely to be incurred by the third parties if the orders sought by the wife were made, are considered later in these reasons.

The economic, legal or other capacity of the third parties to comply with the order

The third parties asserted that the making of the orders sought by the wife would place the second respondent at risk of insolvency, and that the wife’s proposal did not properly account for the ongoing liabilities of the third parties, particularly the second respondent. Insolvency of the second respondent would appear from the evidence before the Court to be at least a potential consequence of the orders proposed by the wife. The Court ought not, as a matter of policy, condone, much less require, actions being taken by the directors of a corporation that would render that corporation insolvent.
It was otherwise submitted that the orders proposed by the wife would compel the directors of the third parties to take steps which were not in the best interests of the companies or their shareholders and may thereby place them in breach of their statutory and fiduciary duties. Notwithstanding the terms of section 90AC of the Act, I accept that this is an important consideration and is a circumstance that should be avoided by the Court where possible.

Other matters raised by the third parties

Likely losses occasioned by third parties

The third parties asserted that the making of the orders sought by the wife would cause them to occasion substantial losses over and above taxation costs, including:
(a) Sale costs, marketing and preparation expenses associated with the sale of the Suburb E property;

(b) Accounting costs associated with the implementation of the orders sought;

(c) The loss of the long-term investment opportunity in terms of capital growth represented by the Suburb E property, which was one of the reasons for its purchase;

(d) Loss of the ability to utilise the Suburb E property as security for lending undertaken by S International Pty Ltd, being the main trading entity in the Capello Group, which was another reason for its purchase. This was a use specifically referred to in the loan agreement relating to the Suburb E property loan, though there is no evidence that the Suburb E property has in fact been used for this purpose at any time since its purchase.

(e) Potential loss of the opportunity to obtain the most favourable sale price for the Suburb E property by reason of the fact that the property is subject to a lease which will not expire until 31 August 2028.

(f) The very real potential for the sale of the Suburb E property to result in a net loss for the second respondent.

Section 90AJ of the Act provides that:
90AJ Expenses of third party

(1) Subsection (2) applies if:
(a) the court has made an order or granted an injunction in accordance with this Part in relation to a marriage; and

(b) a third party in relation to the marriage has incurred expense as a necessary result of the order or injunction.

(2) The court may make such order as it considers just for the payment of the reasonable expenses of the third party incurred as a necessary result of the order or injunction.

(3) In deciding whether to make an order under subsection (2), subject to what the court considers just, the court must take into account the principle that the parties to the marriage should bear the reasonable expenses of the third party equally.

(4) The regulations may provide, in situations where the court has not made an order under subsection (2):

(a) for the charging by the third party of reasonable fees to cover the reasonable expenses of the third party incurred as a necessary result of the order or injunction; and

(b) if such fees are charged—that each of the parties to the marriage is separately liable to pay the third party an amount equal to half of those fees; and

(c) for conferring jurisdiction on a particular court or courts in relation to the collection or recovery of such fees.

Also relevant is regulation 74 of the Family Law Regulations 2024 (Cth), which provides:
Third party expenses
(1) For the purposes of subsection 90AJ(4) of the Act, this section provides for matters relating to the expenses of a third party in relation to a marriage in situations where the court has not made an order under subsection 90AJ(2) of the Act in relation to those expenses.

(2) A third party in relation to a marriage may charge reasonable fees to cover the reasonable expenses of the third party incurred as a necessary result of an order made or an injunction granted, in accordance with Part VIIIAA of the Act, in relation to the marriage.

(3) Without limiting subsection (2), the fees may cover the reasonable expenses incurred by the third party in complying with the order or injunction.

Examples: Expenses incurred for any of the following matters could be covered in the fees charged by the third party:

(a) legal and registration fees;

(b) valuation fees;

(c) government charges and duties;

(d) searching, obtaining and producing documents;

(e) postage, delivery, transport or other transmission of documents;

(f) communications with the parties to the marriage or another person.
(4) Each of the parties to the marriage is separately liable to pay to the third party half of the total amount of the fees charged.
(5) Jurisdiction is conferred on, and invested in, the court:
(a) to decide whether fees charged by a third party under subsection (2) are reasonable; and

(b) to make an order in relation to the collection or recovery of such reasonable fees.

Nonetheless, the same considerations as discussed earlier in these reasons with respect to the indemnity offered by the wife apply in relation to the likelihood of expenses being incurred by the third parties.

Need to declare dividends equally

It was submitted on behalf of the third parties that any order requiring either or both of the second and third respondents to pay a dividend to one of their two shareholders would require an equivalent dividend to be paid to the other shareholder, which would enlarge the extent to which the orders would impact on the broader Capello Group and other family members and would increase the impact of the orders on the third parties themselves.
Subsection 254W(2) of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) (“the Corporations Act”) provides that subject to the terms on which shares in a proprietary company are on issue, the directors may pay dividends as they see fit. This is a replaceable rule and can therefore be displaced or modified by the company's constitution.[76] Clause 1.3 of the constitution of the second respondent (“the constitution”)[77] provides that “to the extent permitted by the Act,[78] the replaceable rules referred to in the Act shall have no application to the Company.”
Senior counsel for the third parties relied on clause 26.1 of the constitution, which provides that “a dividend may only be paid by the Company to the extent permitted by section 254T of the Corporations Act.”
Subsection 254T(1) relevantly provides that a company must not pay a dividend unless:
(a) the company's assets exceed its liabilities immediately before the dividend is declared and the excess is sufficient for the payment of the dividend; and

(b) the payment of the dividend is fair and reasonable to the company's shareholders as a whole; and

(c) the payment of the dividend does not materially prejudice the company's ability to pay its creditors.

A note to that subsection provides that as an example, the payment of a dividend would materially prejudice a company's ability to pay its creditors if the company would become insolvent as a result of the payment.
Senior counsel for the third parties submitted, relying on Industrial Equity Ltd v Blackburn[79] that the declaration of a differential dividend may constitute a breach of subsection 254T(1)(b) and may otherwise constitute oppression within the meaning of section 232 of the Corporations Act. In response, counsel for the wife submitted that although the payment of a differential dividend may constitute a breach of subsection 254T(1)(b), this would not necessarily be the case. Counsel for the wife further submitted that whilst the payment of a differential dividend could constitute oppression, in circumstances in which the shareholders had been present and given the opportunity to be heard at the hearing, this would not be the case.
In my view, it is not necessary to have resort to these provisions at all. The issue is readily resolved by reference to clause 26.7 of the constitution, which provides that “[s]ubject to any rights or restrictions attached to any shares or class of shares, all Dividends must be paid equally on all shares....”
Whilst, as submitted on behalf of the wife, section 90AC of the Act provides that Part VIIIAA has effect despite anything to the contrary in (relevantly) any other Commonwealth law or instrument, this means only that the court could make orders which override the relevant laws and the constitution. No part of the relief sought by the wife would in fact have the effect of overriding clause 26.7 of the constitution of the second respondent or relieving the second respondent of its obligations to the remaining shareholder.
I am satisfied on the basis of clause 26.7 that the submission made on behalf of the third parties is correct insofar as it relates to the second respondent, and that the order sought by the wife with respect to the second respondent would have the effect of requiring the payment of a dividend of equal value to the other shareholder, being S International Pty Ltd.
As to the proposed order for payment of a dividend by the third respondent, the constitution of that company is not in evidence before the Court, and it is therefore not known whether the replaceable rule contained within section 254W of the Corporations Act applies. Although it is arguable that the provisions to which I have referred have the effect that dividends must be declared equally amongst all shareholders of the same class as contended by the third parties, I am not satisfied that this is necessarily so.[80]

Taxation consequences for other parties

In addition to the taxation consequences for the second and third respondents, as outlined above, the making of the orders as sought by the wife would also have potential taxation consequences for the other shareholder of each of them, particularly if requirement for equal payment of dividends applies to the third respondent as it does to the second respondent. There was no evidence before the Court as to, for example, the potential taxation consequences for the husband’s brother, as the other shareholder of the third respondent, were he to receive a dividend from that company.
It was also submitted on behalf of the third parties that the sale of the Suburb E property would itself have consequences for the entire structure, which includes other entities and family members, though this submission was not developed.

CONCLUSION

The husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations and to comply with court orders that are binding upon him, including but not limited to the orders the subject of the present enforcement application, are matters of serious concern to the Court. If these matters are not remedied, they will no doubt feature heavily in the trial in these proceedings, and this may lead to consequences for the husband. Such consequences would be in addition to the payment of interest at the rate provided for under the Rules,[81] which is well above the Reserve Bank of Australia cash rate, is accruing by operation of section 117B of the Act and will continue to do so for so long as the husband’s obligations remain unfulfilled.
The payments required by the terms of the interim orders are not optional. Nor are they “demands” or “requests” made by the wife as the husband sought to characterise them. They are legally binding obligations which must be fulfilled. The Court’s refusal of the specific application presently before it is not, and should not be interpreted as, a determination that the husband’s obligations cannot or will not be enforced. The parties, and particularly the husband, should be under no misapprehension that the failure to comply with binding obligations can entail serious consequences, and that the orders made by Hartnett J remain in force and binding.
I am conscious that the wife’s inability to establish a number of the matters discussed in these reasons has arisen at least in part as a consequence of limitations to the documents and information made available to her, notwithstanding the husband’s disclosure obligations. It is unfortunate that the wife has been hampered, at least in part, in the enforcement of obligations owed to her by the husband, as a result of his conduct. The Court is loath to condone any outcome which permits the consequences of recalcitrance of a party to be visited upon another party.
The Court’s refusal of the wife’s enforcement application similarly should not be taken as an indication that the full extent of the interests of the husband in the Capello Group entities and the estate will not be brought to account in the parties’ ultimate property settlement. The submissions made on behalf of the wife to the effect that the husband is not entitled to hide behind the corporate veil at the expense of the wife are entirely correct.
The husband is required by section 96 of the Act to act consistently with the overarching purpose contained within section 95 in both his conduct of the proceedings and in negotiations for settlement. The overarching purpose involves the just resolution of disputes according to law, as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible; and its objectives include the efficient use of the judicial resources of the Court and the disposal of proceedings in a timely manner at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.
The husband’s obligation to act in accordance with the overarching purpose requires acceptance of the reality that interests held by spouse parties in corporate and other entities will not simply be ignored or overlooked by reason of the fact that third party interests are involved. The question for ultimate determination is not whether the wife will receive a just and equitable settlement but how this may be achieved. The husband should, in accordance with his obligations pursuant to the overarching purpose, direct his attention to how he can work with the wife and the members of the Capello Group to reach an outcome that properly recognises and accounts for his interests in the group, rather than attempting to use the structure of the group as a barrier to the just and equitable distribution of the assets and liabilities of the parties to the marriage, which includes those interests.
Nonetheless, by reason of the combined effect of the matters outlined in these reasons, particularly the following matters, I am not persuaded to grant the relief as sought in the wife’s Amended Enforcement Application:
(a) The order sought by the wife is directed in part to the payment of spousal maintenance rather than an order made under section 79.

(b) There is uncertainty about the applicability of 90AE to an enforcement application.

(c) There is uncertainty about the applicability of 90AE to an interim order.

(d) I am unable to be satisfied that the proposed orders are “reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, to effect a division of property between the parties to the marriage” or “just and equitable” as required by section 90AE in circumstances in which:

(i) there are very real doubts about the utility and proportionality of the orders sought in light of what would appear to be limited prospects that any profit will be derived by sale of the Suburb E property;

(ii) the evidence before the Court about the extent and value husband’s interests in the Capello Group more broadly, other available options for realising his interests in property and the likely impact of the orders is incomplete and uncertain; and

(iii) there might be considerable losses occasioned by the third parties in compliance with the orders.

(e) I am unable to make findings as to important disputed issues such as the allegation of actions having been taken with the goal of defeating the wife’s claim.

(f) There is insufficient evidence to enable the Court to be satisfied that an order of the type sought by the wife would take into account the relevant taxation consequences, particularly those for the third parties.

For all of the reasons outlined above, the wife’s Amended Enforcement Application will be dismissed. There remain before the Court extant interim issues pertaining to costs and questions of joinder and disjoinder. The orders of the Court will provide for the matter to be listed for directions with respect to those issues.
